checks.finding: REQ-007 requires each layer template to contain "at least the diagrams/contracts required by the source plan," but the reviewed specification does not enumerate that source plan's complete diagram/contract set; AC-002 through AC-005 name several examples without stating that they are exhaustive. An implementer or verifier therefore cannot decide whether satisfying AC-002 through AC-005 is sufficient or whether additional source-plan artifacts are mandatory. This belongs to specification review because it defines required product output, not implementation architecture. Major is justified because two implementers can produce materially different template contents and both plausibly claim compliance.

The requirements and acceptance tests consistently preserve explicit feature-mode validation, LITE exclusion, extension of existing review stages without adding a stage, and the Draft-to-Approved governance boundary; no directly conflicting goals or impossible acceptance criteria were found.

REQ-012 requires every feature name to satisfy `^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9-]*$`, with every invalid value producing `invalid feature: <value>`, exit 1, and no access outside `specs/`. The acceptance-test behavioral contract covers only empty, absolute, or traversal-like selectors and does not cover other invalid regex boundaries such as uppercase characters or underscores. A verifier cannot decide from the planned cases whether the complete mandated slug grammar is enforced rather than only traversal prevention. This belongs to specification review because the missing observable negative-path coverage is tied directly to the required input contract. Major is justified because incomplete validation can permit names the requirement explicitly forbids and creates Bash/PowerShell parity risk.

The Assumptions section states that supported hosts preserve Mermaid safely, that implementation-policy and task review loops can be extended without changing ownership or order, and that version `1.3.0` is released so `1.4.0` is next; none is resolved by an investigation artifact or marked as a decision requiring resolution before downstream work. Implementers cannot know whether host compatibility, review-loop extensibility, and the release baseline are established constraints or unverified premises. This belongs to specification review because these premises determine required outputs, workflow scope, and release metadata before design/task decomposition. Major is justified because a false premise could invalidate host behavior, workflow integration, or the mandated release version.

REQ-017 and AC-016 make the governance boundary observable: tasks remain Draft after task review, `implement-task` rejects Draft tasks, and only a human edit or active signed `sdd-sudo` transition may approve selected tasks. The acceptance-test contracts cover both rejection and authorized approval.

REQ-007 leaves the complete mandatory layer-template artifact set dependent on an unenumerated source-plan requirement, while the Assumptions section leaves host Mermaid behavior, review-loop extensibility, and the `1.4.0` release baseline unvalidated and not identified as pending decisions. An implementation-policy reviewer would have to invent whether AC-002 through AC-005 are exhaustive and whether those premises may be treated as established. These are specification-level product, workflow, and release decisions rather than implementation-policy choices. Major is justified because design and task decomposition cannot be made reproducible until those inputs are made self-contained or explicitly resolved.
